title: Cornish fairings
subtitle: Golden-syrup and ginger biscuits from Cornwall
excerpt: Crisp, golden, heavily spiced ginger biscuits that spread into a crackling surface pattern in the oven. A classic of Cornish fairgrounds and market stalls, and simple to make at home.
sourceNotes: Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): Cornish fairings documented as a regional speciality of Cornwall, sold at county fairs. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: ginger biscuit method, the rubbing-in technique and the golden-syrup binding.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked biscuits in a sealed box. Return to room temperature before eating.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in a tin for a week. Improves on day two.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf; biscuits spread and crack quickly — watch from 10 minutes

glossaryTerms.definition: A British spice blend typically containing coriander, cassia, caraway, nutmeg, ginger, and cloves. Used in biscuits, cakes, and fruit puddings. The exact blend varies by brand; a home mix of equal parts of those six spices works.

content.text: A Cornish fairing should be crisp, very gingery, and golden all the way to the centre. The bicarbonate of soda is what creates the surface cracking: as it reacts with the golden syrup during baking, the biscuit spreads and the surface breaks into the distinctive cratered pattern. Do not underbake — these are supposed to be crisp, not chewy.
Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Line two baking trays with baking paper.
, and {{fine-sea-salt}} in a large bowl. Add the cold {{unsalted-butter}} and rub in until the mixture resembles fine breadcrumbs. Stir in the {{caster-sugar}}.

Warm the {{golden-syrup}} briefly in a small saucepan until runny, then add it to the crumb mixture. Mix to a firm dough. Roll into balls the size of a marble — about 15 g each. Place on the trays with plenty of space between them: they spread to roughly twice their raw diameter.

Bake for 10–12 minutes. Watch from 10 minutes: the biscuits should be spread, golden, and crackling on the surface. They will still feel slightly soft; they crisp up as they cool. Slide the paper off the tray onto a rack and leave the biscuits undisturbed for 5 minutes before lifting.
